ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken salad entry; the specific variant with egg and 3 toppings without sauce is not individually coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers chicken salad as a composite dish. The specific egg and 3-topping, no-sauce variant is not individually enumerated; approximate match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chicken salad with egg composite dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, representing a full plate of mixed greens topped with cooked chicken, one hard-boiled egg, and three additional toppings served without sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Salad with Egg (3 Toppings), No Sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific dish (cr_id F08010015). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate mixed salad with protein (chicken and egg) entries from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is very low at 1.65 g/100g, predominantly from vegetables; the GI is expected to be very low. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (15 × 4.95 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.74, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium at 159 mg/100g without sauce; potassium from vegetables supports DASH alignment
hypertension.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed without added sauce; sodium remains at 159 mg per 100 g, and the vegetable base provides potassium that supports a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines — however, patients should ensure chicken is not pre-marinated in high-sodium preparations and should avoid high-sodium toppings such as processed cheese.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 1.65 g/100g and high protein at 7.69 g/100g supporting postprandial glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content of 1.65 g/100g and high protein content of 7.69 g/100g, which together minimise postprandial glucose excursions; patients should avoid high-glycaemic toppings such as corn, croutons, or sweetened dried fruit, and should select toppings from non-starchy vegetables, seeds, or nuts to maintain the low glycaemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat at 2.59 g/100g, low saturated fat estimated at 0.70 g/100g, and high dietary fibre at 1.82 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with total fat of 2.59 g/100g and estimated saturated fat of approximately 0.70 g/100g, well within heart-healthy thresholds; the dietary fibre content of 1.82 g/100g from mixed greens and vegetable toppings supports LDL cholesterol management, and patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid adding high-saturated-fat toppings such as bacon or full-fat cheese.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, meaningful dietary fibre, and antioxidant-rich vegetable toppings support anti-inflammatory status
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is appropriate for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its low saturated fat content, meaningful dietary fibre, and antioxidant-rich vegetable base; patients should consider selecting omega-3-rich toppings such as walnuts or flaxseed to further support anti-inflammatory benefit, and those with nightshade sensitivity should avoid tomatoes or bell peppers as topping choices.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Salad with Egg (3 Toppings), No Sauce is a customisable Western-style salad dish consisting of cooked chicken served over a bed of fresh mixed salad greens, topped with one hard-boiled egg and a choice of three additional toppings, served without any dressing or sauce. The dish is light and refreshing in texture, with the tender cooked chicken providing a savoury protein base, the crisp mixed greens contributing freshness and dietary fibre, and the egg adding richness and additional protein. The absence of sauce keeps the caloric density very low at 61 kcal per 100 g. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its high protein content of 7.69 g/100g, very low carbohydrate content of 1.65 g/100g, and low total fat of 2.59 g/100g, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing weight, blood glucose, or cardiovascular risk. The sodium content of 159 mg/100g is moderate and remains controlled without sauce. Dietary fibre at 1.82 g/100g from the vegetable base supports digestive health and satiety. This dish is classified as halal and gluten-free in its standard preparation. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or light dinner option and is well-suited for individuals following low-carbohydrate, ketogenic, or calorie-controlled dietary patterns. The three-topping customisation allows adaptation for specific dietary needs, with non-starchy vegetables, seeds, and nuts being the most nutritionally appropriate choices for metabolic health.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08010015) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database.
notes: saturated_fat_g value of 0.70 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous chicken salad with egg composition data, as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adaptation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available from the primary approved source for this specific composite food item. GI is an analogous estimate based on the very low carbohydrate content and protein-dominant macronutrient profile; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. This is a Western-style dish served in Singapore; multilingual local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as this dish does not have established local-language names in the Singaporean food culture context — it is referred to by its English name across all languages in Singapore. The dish is classified as a customisable mixed dish; the three toppings are variable and the nutrient values reflect the HPB canonical average composition.
